frameworks.name: Representation Space Traversal Architecture
frameworks.description: A two-system AI architecture where the primary mechanism is explicit traversal of data representation space, rather than treating representation learning as a byproduct of optimisation.

frameworks.name: Data Transformation System
frameworks.description: Travels representation space using reversible graph transformations (translations). Owns and restructures the shared library. Governs layer fusion and splitting. Writes to empirical library only when subgraph occurrence is consistent under converged rules for a given label. Cannot innovate — all transformations reversible. Requires GA-improved rules to produce label-conditioned reproducibility; without rule improvement, consistent subgraph production across images is not achievable.
frameworks.fitness_signal: Label-conditioned reproducibility: given a fixed label, minimize variance in subgraph structures produced across different images with that label. Lower variance = higher fitness. Library entry is a byproduct of GA convergence — subgraphs that consistently appear under converged rules for a given label earn entry.

frameworks.name: Logic Rewriting System
frameworks.description: Improves the travel logic of the data system. Operates irreversibly on rules (innovation permitted). Evolves itself by the same mechanism using match quality as fitness signal. No write access to either library layer or data. Represented as a graph and operated on by the second system. In the base prototype, GA primitives (mutation and crossover on rule graphs) are fixed — self-evolution mechanism is deferred to second system design.
components: GA over rule graphs (mutation + crossover on rule structure)

frameworks.name: Hierarchical Graph Layer Structure
frameworks.description: Multi-layer graph hierarchy where lower layers hold raw structure and primitives, and upper layers hold nodes that represent compressed subgraphs from below. What a node IS defines what layer it belongs to — layer identity is determined by representational scale, not by operation type. Each layer has its own ruleset. Layers can fuse or split. Input layer is immutable. Reversibility is a property of the Data Transformation System as a whole, not a distinguishing property of any particular layer.
components: Layer nodes (each upper-layer node represents a subgraph from the layer below)
Fusion and splitting (governed by data system)
frameworks.layer_boundary_definition: A subgraph from layer N becomes a node at layer N+1 when the GA has converged on rules that consistently produce that subgraph for a given label. The boundary is earned through convergence, not assigned by design.

transformation_types.within_layer: Switches representation of information without changing abstraction level. Example: 3+4 and 7 contain the same information — arithmetic operations are within-layer transformations.
transformation_types.layer_transition: Changes abstraction level — collapses local detail into higher-level structure. Example: full expression tree collapsed into a single result node.

frameworks.name: Hybrid Representation Model
frameworks.description: Previously considered — rejected. Leaf attributes are counter to design philosophy. Complexity is absorbed upward through the layer hierarchy into compressed subgraph nodes, not stored as primitive attributes. Continuous-to-discrete boundary handled at input layer via quantization.

frameworks.name: Human Capability Threshold Gate
frameworks.description: Human evaluates system readiness before unlocking the Reasoned Library. Threshold is NOT delegated to the system. Candidate criteria: accuracy across minimum distinct tasks, generalisation outside training distribution, internal consistency of logic-derived entries validated against labels.

frameworks.name: Base Substrate Architecture
frameworks.description: The graph formalism used by both the Data Transformation System and the second meta-level system. Modular and scalable — base prototype uses directed graphs with binary edge typing. Richer substrates are named upgrade paths evaluated empirically against measured limitations of the base.

decisions.directionality: Directed — all edges are directed. Encodes causality, transformation order, and hierarchy direction.
decisions.edge_types: Exactly two types, globally fixed, no extensions permitted at this level. Type 1: structural — defines what a pattern is (composition, identity). Type 2: operational — defines transformations between patterns, and serves as cursor/pointer in rule firing. Reduces isomorphism complexity by allowing regime-separated matching. Binary typing does not clutter rule space.
decisions.node_typing: None. Typing complicates rule space. Identity emerges from structural relationships and library compression, not labels.
decisions.weights: Not in base prototype. Named upgrade path — to be evaluated empirically.
decisions.hyperedges: Not in base prototype. Named upgrade path — motivated by CA-derived insight that discrete systems have fragmented representation spaces with unreachable configurations. To be evaluated empirically via Phase 6 of prototype roadmap.
decisions.automation: Substrate upgrade decisions are made manually by the researcher at the start of a training run, not automated at runtime.

frameworks.two_level_principle: Every substrate decision must be evaluated twice — once for the traveler operating on data, once for the second system operating on the traveler. Features that only serve one level do not belong in the shared base.

frameworks.name: Graph Rewrite Rule Representation
frameworks.description: Rules are represented as triples of graphs. Graph 1 (match graph): mixed edge types, used for pattern matching against target. Graph 2S (structural transition graph): single structural edge type, specifies structural changes after matched structural edges are stripped. Graph 2O (operational transition graph): single operational edge type, specifies new operational edges to add after transition. Output is always clean — no mixed edges in output. The triple schema is required because Graph 2 must be able to express both structural and operational output edges, and a single-type graph cannot encode both without ambiguity or violating the binary edge constraint.
rule_firing_sequence: 1. Match Graph 1 (mixed) against target graph — produces node mapping from pattern nodes to target nodes
2. Strip one edge type from the matched subgraph (produces clean single-type subgraph)
3. Apply Graph 2S (structural transition) to the cleaned subgraph
4. Apply Graph 2O (operational transition) to add new operational edges
5. Reattach stripped edges to output nodes via node mapping
frameworks.node_mapping: Explicit mapping from input nodes to output nodes in the rule. Required for reattachment of stripped edges and for applying Graph 2O correctly. Operational edges serve as node pointers in the rule — when input is an operational graph, structural edges take the pointer role instead (role swap handled at match time by interpreter).
frameworks.edge_role_symmetry: Structural and operational edge roles are symmetric and positional, not fundamental. Role swap is an interpreter convention, not a change to rule structure. GA operates on each graph independently — no pairing complexity at GA level.
frameworks.ga_compatibility: Triple schema keeps each graph uniform for GA operations. Mutation and crossover operate on Graph 1, Graph 2S, and Graph 2O independently.
frameworks.bias_testing_note: For early bias testing only: Graph 2O can be omitted and new operational edges hardcoded as a post-processing step in the interpreter. This is valid only while testing matching and structural rewrite machinery in isolation. Must be removed before P5 — hardcoded operational edge logic is imperative code smuggled through the back door and becomes permanent scaffolding if left in.

frameworks.name: Graph Matching Design
frameworks.description: Pattern matching is subgraph isomorphism over directed graphs with binary edge types. The match graph (Graph 1) contains both structural and operational edges. Matching finds a mapping from pattern nodes to target graph nodes such that all pattern edges exist in the target under the mapping, preserving edge type and direction.
frameworks.algorithm: Subgraph isomorphism via backtracking search with constraint propagation. Complexity is O(n!) in worst case — acceptable for small graphs in prototype, flagged for profiling at P6.
frameworks.edge_type_handling: Matching is edge-type-aware. A structural edge in the pattern must map to a structural edge in the target; an operational edge in the pattern must map to an operational edge in the target. This halves the effective search space by separating structural and operational constraint satisfaction.
frameworks.role_swap: At match time, the interpreter determines which edge type plays which role based on the input graph composition. If the input graph is predominantly operational (e.g. a rule graph being operated on by the second system), structural edges take the pointer role and operational edges take the composition role. The swap is an interpreter convention applied before matching begins — the matching algorithm itself is role-agnostic.
frameworks.match_result: A match result is a bijective node mapping: pattern_node -> target_node. Multiple valid mappings may exist (match_all). The mapping is the basis for rule application — Graph 2S and Graph 2O are applied using this mapping to locate the correct target nodes.
frameworks.match_all: match_all() returns all valid mappings. Required for rules that fire repeatedly at different positions in the same graph (e.g. tombstone GC, carry propagation). The traversal loop in P5 calls match_all and applies rules iteratively until no matches remain.
frameworks.negative_constraints: Current match() has no support for negative structural constraints (matching nodes with no incoming edges, or absence of a specific edge). This is a known limitation. Workaround: use tombstone markers to make absence conditions positive and matchable.
frameworks.complexity_flag: O(n!) worst case. For the prototype scope (small arithmetic expressions), this is acceptable. Flagged for P6 profiling — if graph size grows enough that match() dominates runtime, pruning heuristics or VF2 algorithm should be evaluated.

frameworks.name: Graph Operation Interface
frameworks.description: Abstraction layer over graph operations. The traveler and second system interact with graphs only through this interface — no substrate assumptions above it. Enables substrate swapping without touching core logic.

transformation.note: Reversibility guaranteed at interface level regardless of substrate.

substrate_introspection.description: Capability reporting only — upgrade decisions are manual.

frameworks.name: Second System (Meta-level)
frameworks.description: Represents the traveler as a graph and evolves it using graph rewriting — the same substrate and interface as the object level. Currently open in design. Uses the same graph operation interface as the Data Transformation System.
frameworks.notes: Design deliberately deferred. The traveler's actual behavior should inform the second system's design rather than prescribing it upfront. Self-similarity across levels is a goal — same formalism at both object and meta level.

notes: Migrated from _archive/concepts.frameworks.json. Full content preserved. Session 8 notes: Data Transformation System updated with GA requirement and fitness signal. Layer identity corrected to representational scale. Layer boundary definition added. Session 11 notes: Graph Rewrite Rule Representation framework added. Layer transformation types clarified (within-layer vs layer transition). Edge type descriptions updated with cursor/pointer role. Session 2026-05-06 notes: Rule schema updated from pair to triple (Graph 1, Graph 2S, Graph 2O) — required to express mixed-type output without violating binary edge constraint. Bias testing note added. Graph Matching Design framework added — covers subgraph isomorphism, edge-type-aware matching, role swap, match_all, negative constraint limitation, complexity flag.
